questions.q: Identify the rate of developing software that characterises the Agile approach, using the exact syllabus phrasing.
options: Incremental and continuous, delivering working software frequently
Sequential and stage-gated, with formal sign-off between phases
Front-loaded, with all value delivered in one drop at the end
Documentation-driven, with comprehensive specifications signed off up front
questions.explanation: Agile develops software iteratively and incrementally, so its rate of developing is incremental and continuous — working software is delivered frequently in short cycles rather than in one big drop. Option B and option C describe Waterfall ('Waterfall Falls once'), where value arrives sequentially and all at the end, not Agile's looping delivery.

questions.q: What is the correct three-part template for writing an Agile user story?
options: "Given [context], when [event], then [outcome]."
"As a [type of user], I want [some functionality], so that [some benefit]."
"The system shall [requirement] under [condition] with [constraint]."
"In order to [goal], the developer must [task] before [deadline]."
questions.explanation: A user story captures a feature from the user's point of view in the fixed template 'As a [role], I want [functionality], so that [benefit]' — the role, the want, and the why. Option A is the Given-When-Then format used for acceptance criteria or test scenarios, not the user story structure itself, which is the classic exam trap.

questions.q: What does the P-S-R-R mnemonic stand for in the context of Agile ceremonies?
options: Plan, Sprint, Release, Refactor
Prioritise, Standup, Refine, Report
Plan, Standup, Review, Retro
Product, Sprint, Roles, Responsibilities
questions.explanation: P-S-R-R names the four ceremonies: Plan (sprint planning), Standup (the daily fifteen-minute sync), Review (demonstrating the increment to stakeholders), and Retro (the retrospective on the team's process). Option A is tempting because 'Sprint' and 'Release' sound Agile, but the ceremonies are specifically the structured meetings — Plan, Standup, Review, Retro — not the artefacts.

questions.q: Outline what it means for an Agile sprint to be 'time-boxed'.
options: The sprint can be extended until all committed work is finished
The sprint length is recalculated each cycle based on the team's velocity
The sprint has a fixed length that cannot be extended; if work isn't done, the sprint still ends and unfinished work returns to the queue
The sprint must be completed within a single working day to maintain momentum
questions.explanation: Time-boxed means the iteration has a fixed length, typically two weeks, that cannot be extended — when the box closes, the sprint ends regardless of progress, and unfinished work goes back into the backlog. Option A inverts this: extending the sprint to fit the work defeats the steady, predictable rhythm that time-boxing exists to create.

questions.q: Distinguish the purpose of the sprint review from the purpose of the sprint retrospective.
options: The review estimates story points; the retrospective assigns tasks to developers
The review surfaces blockers; the retrospective demonstrates working software
The review demonstrates the working product to stakeholders for feedback; the retrospective reflects on improving the team's own process
The review and retrospective both demonstrate the product, but to different audiences
questions.explanation: The distinction the exam loves: the Review is about the PRODUCT — demoing the working increment to stakeholders and gathering feedback — while the Retro is about the PROCESS — the team reflecting on what to start, stop, and continue to improve how it works. Option B swaps the standup's job (surfacing blockers) into the review, and confuses the two ceremonies the marker most wants you to keep separate.

questions.q: Explain why the statement 'Agile means no planning and no documentation' is incorrect.
options: Agile actually produces more documentation than Waterfall, just later in the project
Agile replaces all documentation with automated tests, so written records are unnecessary
The statement is correct for Scrum but wrong for Kanban
Agile values working software over comprehensive documentation, but 'over' does not mean 'instead of' — it still plans every sprint and documents lightly and continuously
questions.explanation: Agile values working software over comprehensive documentation and responding to change over following a plan, but 'over' is a preference, not a replacement — it plans every single sprint and documents lightly and continuously rather than in giant upfront tomes. In an exam you should write 'Agile uses lighter, evolving documentation produced as needed', never 'Agile has no documentation', which is the trap that loses marks.

questions.q: Define 'method tailoring' as the term is used in the Agile syllabus content.
options: Following the Scrum framework exactly as written to guarantee consistency
Adapting Agile practices, ceremonies, and frameworks to fit a particular team, project, and context rather than applying one framework rigidly
Customising the user-interface styling of software to match a client's brand
Dropping any ceremony or practice a team finds inconvenient during a sprint
questions.explanation: Method tailoring means adapting Agile practices — such as shortening sprints, adjusting ceremonies, or blending Scrum's sprints with Kanban's continuous flow — to suit the team, project, and context instead of following one recipe rigidly. Option D is the misconception to avoid: tailoring requires judgement and is a strength of Agile's flexibility, not a licence to abandon discipline.

questions.q: Compare Agile and Waterfall on how each handles client feedback and change.
options: Both gather feedback only near the end, but Agile makes changes cheaper to apply
Waterfall gathers feedback continuously while Agile freezes requirements after planning
Waterfall gets client feedback late and resists change once a phase is signed off; Agile gets feedback every sprint and embraces change at each iteration
Both embrace change equally, differing only in how much documentation they produce
questions.explanation: Waterfall gathers client feedback late, near the end, and resists change once a phase is signed off, so late changes trigger expensive backflows; Agile gathers feedback every sprint review and embraces change at every iteration. This contrast across feedback timing and change-handling is captured by the paired hooks 'Waterfall Falls once' (one pass, late feedback) and 'Agile Goes around' (looping, continuous feedback).

questions.q: During sprint one of building a school portal, a developer says at the daily standup that she is blocked waiting on the database schema. According to the episode, what should happen with this blocker?
options: The team solves the schema problem during the standup before moving on
The blocker is surfaced at the standup so it can be addressed and unblocked the same day, but it is not solved inside the meeting
The blocker is recorded and deferred to the sprint retrospective for discussion
The sprint is paused and extended until the schema dependency is resolved
questions.explanation: The daily standup's job is to surface blockers fast, not to solve them in the meeting — in the worked example the dependency is flagged and then unblocked the same day outside the fifteen-minute sync. Option A is the common mistake: the standup is a strict fifteen-minute synchronisation, so problem-solving happens afterwards, keeping the meeting short.

questions.q: A start-up is building a consumer-facing app whose requirements are expected to shift frequently as it gathers user feedback. Recommend the more appropriate development approach and justify your choice against the project's defining condition.
options: Waterfall, because its comprehensive upfront documentation and stage-gates make the project predictable and auditable
Waterfall, because freezing requirements after sign-off prevents costly scope changes during development
Agile, because its iterative time-boxed sprints embrace change at every iteration and surface it through continuous feedback, suiting evolving requirements
Either approach works equally well, since both deliver the same software in the end
questions.explanation: The defining condition is rapidly changing requirements, so Agile is more appropriate: its iterative sprints embrace change at every iteration and surface it through continuous sprint-review feedback, whereas Waterfall would force costly backflows each time a requirement shifts. Option A is a true description of Waterfall's strengths, but predictability and documentation are not what this project needs — matching the recommendation to the changing-requirements condition is what earns the marks.
